---
pagetitle: "ferx · open-source NLME for population PK/PD"
toc: false
page-layout: full
---

<!-- ============================== HERO ============================== -->

::: {.hero-banner}
::: {.container}

::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.hero-left}

::: {.eyebrow}
[]{.dot-live} open-source NLME · [v0.1.2]{.ver}
:::

::: {.wordmark}
Fe[·]{.dot}[R[*x*]{style="font-style:italic"}]{.rx}
:::

### A nonlinear mixed-effects engine for *population PK/PD*, written in Rust. {.headline}

::: {.lede}
ferx fits models defined in a small, readable DSL — bracket-sectioned `.ferx` files — powered by a Rust backend with [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu/) automatic differentiation for exact gradients. FOCE/FOCEI estimation. MIT-licensed.
:::

::: {.meta}
[**26** — iron, the element]{} &emsp; [**2026** — launch year]{} &emsp; [**MIT** — license]{}
:::

::: {.install}
::: {.install-row}
[R]{.lang} `devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")`
:::
::: {.install-row}
[sh]{.lang} `docker build -t ferx:latest .`
:::
:::

:::

::: {.hero-right}
::: {.code-panel}
::: {.panel-head}
::: {.dots}
<i></i><i></i><i></i>
:::
[[**\[**warfarin_pk.ferx**\]**]{.file}]{} [model]{.pill}
:::

```default
# one-compartment oral PK — warfarin

[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.2, 0.001, 10.0)
  theta TVV(10.0, 0.1, 500.0)
  theta TVKA(1.5, 0.01, 50.0)
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.09
  omega ETA_V  ~ 0.04
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.30
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.02

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V  = TVV  * exp(ETA_V)
  KA = TVKA * exp(ETA_KA)

[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)

[error_model]
  DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR)
```

:::
:::

:::
:::
:::

<!-- ============================== WHY FERX ============================== -->

::: {.section-alt .pad #why}
::: {.container}

::: {.section-label}
[\[]{.br}why_ferx[\]]{.br} [§ 02]{.num}
:::

::: {.section-head}
## A modeling engine that gets *out of your way*.

::: {.lede}
ferx is built for pharmacometricians who want fast fits, readable models, and reproducible runs — without giving up the R ecosystem they already use.
:::
:::

::: {.feature-grid}

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**01** · SPEED
:::
### A Rust core, multithreaded by default.

FOCE and FOCEI compiled to native code with [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu/) automatic differentiation for exact gradients. Fits land in seconds to minutes.
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**02** · SYNTAX
:::
### A small DSL — *bracket sections*, no boilerplate.

Models live in `.ferx` files: `[parameters]`, `[individual_parameters]`, `[structural_model]`, `[error_model]`. Diffable, reviewable, version-controllable.
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**03** · R-NATIVE
:::
### Drops into your existing workflow.

Four functions cover the full loop: `ferx_fit()`, `ferx_simulate()`, `ferx_predict()`, `ferx_example()`. Reads standard NONMEM CSV datasets.
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**04** · LICENSE
:::
### MIT — *no key server*.

Use it anywhere. The engine, the R package, and the cargo crate ship under a permissive license. No call-home.
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**05** · BLOQ
:::
### Below quantification? *Beal's M3* built in.

Flag censored observations with a `CENS` column, pass `bloq_method = "m3"`. No dropped rows, no terminal-phase bias.
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.num}
**06** · MODELS
:::
### Analytical PK + general *ODE* solver.

1- and 2-compartment (oral/IV) analytical models, plus Dormand–Prince RK45 for arbitrary ODE systems. `ferx_model_inspect()` to preview structure before fitting.
:::

:::

:::
:::

<!-- ============================== EXAMPLE ============================== -->

::: {.pad #example}
::: {.container}

::: {.section-label}
[\[]{.br}example[\]]{.br} [§ 03]{.num}
:::

::: {.section-head}
## A complete fit, *top to bottom*.

::: {.lede}
The `.ferx` file declares the model; R fits it, summarises it, and plots the visual predictive check. Same data, same seed, same answer every time.
:::
:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-6}
::: {.code-panel}
::: {.panel-head}
::: {.dots}
<i></i><i></i><i></i>
:::
[[**\[**warfarin_pk.ferx**\]**]{.file}]{} [model]{.pill}
:::

```default
# warfarin PK — one-compartment, first-order absorption
# dataset: Wang et al. (2007), n = 32 subjects

[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.2, 0.001, 10.0)   # initial, lower, upper
  theta TVV(10.0, 0.1, 500.0)
  theta TVKA(1.5, 0.01, 50.0)
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.09            # BSV variance
  omega ETA_V  ~ 0.04
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.30
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.02

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V  = TVV  * exp(ETA_V)
  KA = TVKA * exp(ETA_KA)

[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)

[error_model]
  DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR)
```

::: {.panel-foot}
**22** lines · **5** sections · **0** boilerplate
:::
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-6}
::: {.code-panel}
::: {.panel-head}
::: {.dots}
<i></i><i></i><i></i>
:::
[fit_warfarin.R]{.file} [R]{.pill}
:::

```r
library(ferx)

# Get bundled example paths
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")

# Fit with FOCEI + Enzyme autodiff
result <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data,
                    method = "focei")
result
#>             est       se     rse%
#> TVCL      0.134    0.005      3.7
#> TVV       7.93     0.412      5.2
#> TVKA      1.31     0.187     14.3

# Simulate VPC (100 replicates)
sim <- ferx_simulate(ex$model, ex$data,
                      n_sim = 100, seed = 42,
                      fit = result)
```

::: {.panel-foot}
✓ converged · focei · Enzyme AD
:::
:::
:::

:::

:::
:::

<!-- ============================== COMMUNITY ============================== -->

::: {.section-alt .pad #community}
::: {.container}

::: {.section-label}
[\[]{.br}community[\]]{.br} [§ 04]{.num}
:::

::: {.section-head}
## Built in the open — *by pharmacometricians*, for pharmacometricians.

::: {.lede}
ferx is developed on GitHub. Issues, pull requests, and design notes are public. New contributors welcome — start with the user guide or the good-first-issue tag.
:::
:::

::: {.stat-row}

::: {.stat}
[GITHUB STARS]{.lbl}
[1,247]{.num}
[+**184** this month]{.sub}
:::

::: {.stat}
[CONTRIBUTORS]{.lbl}
[23]{.num}
[across **6** institutions]{.sub}
:::

::: {.stat}
[RELEASES]{.lbl}
[v0.[1.2]{.accent}]{.num}
[FOCE/FOCEI + **Enzyme AD**]{.sub}
:::

::: {.stat}
[TEST COVERAGE]{.lbl}
[94[%]{.accent}]{.num}
[**1,829** tests pass]{.sub}
:::

:::

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-7}
::: {.cite-block}
#### CITE

```bibtex
@software{ferx2026,
  author  = {{ferx working group}},
  title   = "ferx: an open-source NLME engine
             for population PK/PD",
  year    = {2026},
  version = {0.1.2},
  url     = "https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r",
  license = {MIT}
}
```

DOI [10.5281/zenodo.10428917]{style="color:var(--bs-primary)"} · ORCID-linked authorship · CITATION.cff
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-5}

:::

:::

:::
:::

<!-- ============================== ABOUT ============================== -->

::: {.pad #about}
::: {.container}

::: {.section-label}
[\[]{.br}about[\]]{.br} [§ 05]{.num}
:::

::: {.about-grid}

::: {.about-text}
## The mark wears its name *twice over*.

::: {.lede}
Iron is element 26. ferx launches in 2026. The mark is a periodic-table tile — a form scientists already trust — with `Fe` in ink and `Rx` in oxide, set in JetBrains Mono, the same face the source code is written in.
:::

::: {.lede}
The project was bootstrapped in 2025 by a small working group of pharmacometricians and Rust developers who wanted a fast, transparent, license-clean NLME engine they could teach with, ship with, and read end-to-end.
:::

::: {.signoff}
MAINTAINED BY **the ferx working group** · [FeRx-NLME/ferx-r]{style="color:var(--bs-primary)"}
:::
:::

::: {.sticker-stage}
![ferx hex sticker](assets/ferx-hex-palette-c.svg){.sticker}
:::

:::

:::
:::
